revision.comment: Text replacement - "List of Kujūku Ōji (印南町)" to "List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)"
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 印南町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] ==Overview== == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 404001300X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Tracing the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpu Shobō, ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii'', Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}
: ({{nihongo|Ueno Ōji|上野王子}}) – '''Tsu'i Ōji''' – '''Ikaruga Ōji''' – '''Kirime Ōji''' – '''Kirime Nakayama Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Iwashiro Ōji|岩代王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/nakayamaouji.html Nakayama Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/kanoouji.html Kano Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/ikarugaouji.html Ikaruga Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "==Overview==↵==" to "=="
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 印南町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 404001300X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Tracing the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpu Shobō, ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii'', Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}
: ({{nihongo|Ueno Ōji|上野王子}}) – '''Tsu'i Ōji''' – '''Ikaruga Ōji''' – '''Kirime Ōji''' – '''Kirime Nakayama Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Iwashiro Ōji|岩代王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/nakayamaouji.html Nakayama Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/kanoouji.html Kano Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/ikarugaouji.html Ikaruga Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site]
{{Shinto-stub}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 404001300X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Tracing the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpu Shobō, ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii'', Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}
: ({{nihongo|Ueno Ōji|上野王子}}) – '''Tsu'i Ōji''' – '''Ikaruga Ōji''' – '''Kirime Ōji''' – '''Kirime Nakayama Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Iwashiro Ōji|岩代王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/nakayamaouji.html Nakayama Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/kanoouji.html Kano Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/ikarugaouji.html Ikaruga Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site]

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 404001300X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Tracing the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpu Shobō, ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii'', Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}
: ({{nihongo|Ueno Ōji|上野王子}}) – '''Tsu'i Ōji''' – '''Ikaruga Ōji''' – '''Kirime Ōji''' – '''Kirime Nakayama Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Iwashiro Ōji|岩代王子}})

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] to [[Category:Completion tier 1]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 404001300X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Tracing the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpu Shobō, ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii'', Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}
: ({{nihongo|Ueno Ōji|上野王子}}) – '''Tsu'i Ōji''' – '''Ikaruga Ōji''' – '''Kirime Ōji''' – '''Kirime Nakayama Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Iwashiro Ōji|岩代王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/nakayamaouji.html Nakayama Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/kanoouji.html Kano Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/ikarugaouji.html Ikaruga Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 404001300X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Tracing the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpu Shobō, ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii'', Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}
: ({{nihongo|Ueno Ōji|上野王子}}) – '''Tsu'i Ōji''' – '''Ikaruga Ōji''' – '''Kirime Ōji''' – '''Kirime Nakayama Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Iwashiro Ōji|岩代王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/nakayamaouji.html Nakayama Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/kanoouji.html Kano Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/ikarugaouji.html Ikaruga Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site]

[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks on groups]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]] ]] [[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]][[zh:九十九王子 (印南町)]] [[Category:ppp]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Inami Town)]][[Category:11]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[ja:Inami Town)]]

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>

[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks on groups]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Inami Town)]][[Category:qq]] ]] [[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]][[zh:九十九王子 (印南町)]] [[Category:ppp]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Inami Town)]][[Category:11]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[ja:Inami Town)]] [[Category:New]]

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 404001300X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Tracing the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpu Shobō, ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii'', Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}
: ({{nihongo|Ueno Ōji|上野王子}}) – '''Tsu'i Ōji''' – '''Ikaruga Ōji''' – '''Kirime Ōji''' – '''Kirime Nakayama Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Iwashiro Ōji|岩代王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/nakayamaouji.html Nakayama Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/kanoouji.html Kano Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/ikarugaouji.html Ikaruga Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site]

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
[[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]]
[[Category:qq-Inami Town)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 404001300X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Tracing the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpu Shobō, ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii'', Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}
: ({{nihongo|Ueno Ōji|上野王子}}) – '''Tsu'i Ōji''' – '''Ikaruga Ōji''' – '''Kirime Ōji''' – '''Kirime Nakayama Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Iwashiro Ōji|岩代王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/nakayamaouji.html Nakayama Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/kanoouji.html Kano Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/ikarugaouji.html Ikaruga Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site]
{{Shinto-stub}}

These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Inami Town)-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]]
[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Inami Town)-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Inami Town)-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]]
[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Inami Town)-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 404001300X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Tracing the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpu Shobō, ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii'', Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}
: ({{nihongo|Ueno Ōji|上野王子}}) – '''Tsu'i Ōji''' – '''Ikaruga Ōji''' – '''Kirime Ōji''' – '''Kirime Nakayama Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Iwashiro Ōji|岩代王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/nakayamaouji.html Nakayama Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/kanoouji.html Kano Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/ikarugaouji.html Ikaruga Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site]

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Inami Town)-->
[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Inami Town)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 404001300X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Tracing the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpu Shobō, ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii'', Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}
: ({{nihongo|Ueno Ōji|上野王子}}) – '''Tsu'i Ōji''' – '''Ikaruga Ōji''' – '''Kirime Ōji''' – '''Kirime Nakayama Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Iwashiro Ōji|岩代王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/nakayamaouji.html Nakayama Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/kanoouji.html Kano Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/ikarugaouji.html Ikaruga Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site]
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Inami Town|ja|印南町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Inami Town)-->
[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Inami Town)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (印南町)]] == Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami, Wakayama == The term '''Kujūku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a network of {{nihongo|Shinto shrines|神社|jinja}} along the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes, particularly along the {{nihongo|Kiiji|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} and {{nihongo|Nakahechi|中辺路}} routes in {{nihongo|Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県}}. These shrines were organized primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{nihongo|Kumano Shugendō|熊野修験道}} practitioners to provide spiritual protection for pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility during their pilgrimages to the {{nihongo|Kumano Sanzan|熊野三山}}.
After the {{nihongo|Jōkyū War|承久の乱}} in 1221, pilgrimages from Kyoto declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kiiji route and the associated shrines. This trend accelerated during the {{nihongo|Muromachi period|室町時代}}, and despite efforts by the {{nihongo|Kishū Domain|紀州藩}} during the {{nihongo|Edo period|江戸時代}} to revive the pilgrimage culture, many shrines fell into disrepair. The rise of {{nihongo|State Shinto|国家神道}} in the {{nihongo|Meiji period|明治時代}} further contributed to the consolidation or disappearance of many of these shrines.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines located within {{nihongo|Inami Town|印南町}}, Wakayama Prefecture.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines in Inami == Inami Town is home to four Kujūku Ōji shrines:
=== Tsu'i Ōji (津井王子) === Tsu'i Ōji, also known as '''吐王子''' (Tsu'i Ōji), is mentioned in the ''{{nihongo|Kumano Michi no Ma Guki|熊野道之間愚記}}'' (1201) as ''Tsui no Ōji''. It was relocated to the area from the nearby Tsu'i district, although its original site remains uncertain. In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine, with only a stone monument marking its former location today.
* Location: Inami 745, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Ikaruga Ōji (斑鳩王子) === Ikaruga Ōji, also known as '''Tomino Ōji''' (富ノ王子), is mentioned in historical records dating back to 1109. The shrine was believed to have housed a sacred stone as its object of worship. Merged into Yamaguchi Hachiman Shrine in 1908, it was later reestablished in 1950 at its current location.
* Location: Inami 3450, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kirime Ōji (切目王子) === {{Main|{{ill|Kirime-ōji|fr|4=ja|5=切目王子}}}} Kirime Ōji is one of the most significant shrines in Inami, recognized as part of the Five Major Ōji Shrines (五体王子). It has been designated a Prefectural Historic Site since 1958.
=== Kirime Nakayama Ōji (切目中山王子) === Located southeast of Kirime Ōji, Nakayama Ōji Shrine is thought to have been relocated from its original site based on descriptions in the ''Kumano Michi no Ma Guki''. Two stone pagodas from the early Muromachi period stand in front of the shrine, designated as municipal cultural properties.
* Location: Shimada 2916, Inami Town, Wakayama Prefecture
== Nearby Sites == The trail from Kirime Nakayama Ōji to the next shrine, Iwashiro Ōji in Minabe Town, passes through the Enoki Pass (榎木峠), preserving the ancient atmosphere of the Kumano Kōdō.
<gallery> File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 1.jpg|Path to Enoki Pass File:Kiiji - Enoki pass.jpg|Enoki Pass File:Kiiji ancient road in Inami, Wakayama 2.jpg|Trail beyond Enoki Pass </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten'' Editorial Committee (1985), ''Wakayama Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, ISBN 404001300X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), ''Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Tracing the Kujūku Ōji Shrines'', Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpu Shobō, ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha Editorial (1997), ''Encyclopedia of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii'', Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}
: ({{nihongo|Ueno Ōji|上野王子}}) – '''Tsu'i Ōji''' – '''Ikaruga Ōji''' – '''Kirime Ōji''' – '''Kirime Nakayama Ōji''' – ({{nihongo|Iwashiro Ōji|岩代王子}})
== External Links == * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/nakayamaouji.html Nakayama Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/kanoouji.html Kano Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site] * [http://www.town.wakayama-inami.lg.jp/kyoiku/ikarugaouji.html Ikaruga Ōji Site – Inami Town Official Site]
